title: Declaration and binding visual grammar
summary: Recognized declarations move the binding before its type, distinguish inferred from explicit types, and make mutable, constant, copied, or uninitialized state visually loud.
details: A mutable local int x{7} becomes mut x: int = 7; a const local becomes x: int = 7.
An inferred mutable local auto x = value becomes mut x := value; const auto becomes x := value.
Explicit type initialization uses equals, inferred type initialization uses colon-equals, and constexpr replaces equals with a second colon.
Members do not receive mut merely for being assignable object state; mutable reference members do receive mut because they expose a mutable borrow.
Uninitialized members and arrays use a red no_init prefix; uninitialized scalar locals remain raw because deferred initialization is a common local idiom.
Structured bindings and range-for bindings retain reference/pointer sigils on the binding names and surface mutability.

Declaration runs align mut, names, types, and initializers with inline padding; bare uninitialized C/C++ struct fields participate in the same contiguous block as pointer/reference fields rather than forming accidental sub-runs.
Ambiguous or unsupported forms such as multi-declarators, function pointers, incomplete multiline right-hand sides, and most-vexing-parse function declarations remain raw.

provenance.note: Parsing/classification, chunk construction, alignment, and overlay application implement this grammar.

evidence.reference: tests/frontend_spec.lua declaration, auto, members, alignment, and edge-case sections
evidence.claim: The supported grammar and explicit raw fallbacks are assertion-driven.

questions: How are explicit and inferred declarations distinguished?
Why do members and locals receive different mut markers?
Do bare scalar and pointer fields align in one C struct block?

last_verified.method: Current parse/render/view code matched against focused, C ABI interaction, and golden declaration tests.
